naturalMeaning: The reading shifts with some digits — 300 is さんびゃく (sanbyaku), 600 ろっぴゃく (roppyaku), 800 はっぴゃく (happyaku), not the plain ひゃく.
Japanese groups large numbers by 10,000 (万), not 1,000 — so 100,000 is 十万 (juuman, 'ten man'), which trips up English speakers used to thinking in thousands.

examples.reading: これはごひゃくえんです。

naturalMeaning: Doubles as 'how many (things)' and, with お-, a polite 'how old' — おいくつですか is a gentler age question than 何歳ですか.
Attaches to a number for clock time: 三時 (sanji, 3:00). A few readings are irregular — 4:00 is よじ (yoji), not よんじ; 9:00 is くじ (kuji).
Reading alternates between ふん and ぷん depending on the number: 一分 いっぷん, 三分 さんぷん, 四分 よんぷん.

examples.text: 毎日日本語を勉強します。
examples.reading: まいにちにほんごをべんきょうします。

naturalMeaning: Means 'time' as a resource (時間がない, 'no time') and also counts hours (三時間, 'three hours') — distinct from 時 (ji), which is clock o'clock.

examples.text: いつ日本に行きますか。
examples.reading: いつにほんにいきますか。
examples.translation: When are you going to Japan?
How is Japan? / What do you think of Japan?

naturalMeaning: Neutral 'why'. なぜ (naze) is more formal/written; casual speech often uses なんで (nande).
Stands alone as 'this thing'. To put it before a noun you need この instead (この本, 'this book') — これ can't directly modify a noun.
The こ/そ/あ system maps to distance: これ (near me), それ (near you), あれ (away from us both).

examples.text: この店はおいしいです。
examples.reading: このみせはおいしいです。

naturalMeaning: The same kanji 人 reads じん (jin) in nationalities (日本人 nihonjin) and にん (nin) as the people-counter (三人 sannin), but ひと as the standalone word for 'person'.
A special reading (jukujikun): the kanji 大 and 人 don't individually say おとな — the whole word is read as a unit.
Also a title of address for teachers, doctors, and other experts — used after a name (田中先生) and never about yourself.
Japanese distinguishes older from younger siblings as separate words. 兄 is your own older brother to others; お兄さん addresses him or refers to someone else's.
頭がいい (atama ga ii) literally 'head is good' means 'smart' — a very common set expression.
Homophone with 花 (hana, 'flower') — same sound はな, different kanji and pitch accent.
足 covers both foot and leg — Japanese doesn't split them into separate everyday words the way English does.
お腹が空きました (onaka ga sukimashita), literally 'my stomach emptied', is the normal way to say 'I'm hungry'.
From Portuguese 'pão', not English — one of the oldest loanwords, dating to 16th-century Portuguese traders.
From Dutch 'bier', another early European loanword predating widespread English contact.
Means alcohol in general, not only rice wine — 'do you drink?' is お酒を飲みますか.
The kanji word for cow's milk. The katakana ミルク (miruku) is also common, especially for milk in coffee.
Often written お寿司 with the polite お-, and the す can voice to ず in compounds (にぎり寿司 → nigirizushi).
Usually お弁当 with the polite お-. Convenience-store bentou (コンビニ弁当) are a daily-life staple.
Literally 'morning rice' — 朝 (morning) + ご飯 (rice/meal). The same pattern gives 昼ご飯 (lunch) and 晩ご飯 (dinner).
The noun 'red'. As an adjective describing a red thing it becomes 赤い (akai) — most basic colors have both a noun and an い-adjective form.
Historically covered blue and green both — a green traffic light is still called 青 (ao), and fresh greenery can be 青い.
Literally 'tea color' (茶 tea + 色 color).
Only for objects, never people — an old person is 年を取った (toshi o totta) or お年寄り, and calling a person 古い would sound wrong.
One word for both 'expensive' and 'tall/high' — context decides. Its opposites split: 安い (yasui) for cheap, 低い (hikui) for low.
早い is 'early' (time); 速い, same reading, is 'fast' (speed). Both are はやい in speech, distinguished only by kanji.
暑い is hot weather; 熱い (same reading あつい) is a hot object or liquid — two kanji, one sound.
寒い is for cold weather/air. A cold object or drink is 冷たい (tsumetai) instead.
Also describes a cold, unkind manner in a person (冷たい人, 'a cold person').
優しい is kind/gentle in personality; 易しい, same reading やさしい, means 'easy' — distinguished by kanji.
Covers both 'interesting' and 'amusing/funny' — a book, a class, and a joke can all be 面白い.
A な-adjective. 元気ですか is 'are you well?', and 元気な子供 is 'an energetic child' (note the な before the noun).
A な-adjective despite ending in い — the い is part of the root, not the adjective ending. Means both 'beautiful' and 'clean/tidy'.
A な-adjective and also a noun. 暇ですか ('are you free?') is a normal, non-rude way to ask if someone has time.
Does double duty — 音楽を聞く (listen to music) and 道を聞く (ask directions). Context makes clear which.

examples.text: コンビニでお茶を買います。
examples.reading: コンビニでおちゃをかいます。
examples.translation: I buy tea at the convenience store.

naturalMeaning: Takes に, not を, for the person met: 友達に会います ('I meet a friend'), because you 'meet with' someone.

examples.reading: まいあさろくじにおきます。
examples.translation: I get up at 6:00 every morning.

naturalMeaning: Not just child's play — adults use 遊ぶ for hanging out or going out for fun (友達と遊ぶ, 'hang out with friends').

translation: to rest / take a day off

naturalMeaning: A noun (勉強, 'study') plus する — one of countless する-verbs. 勉強します is the polite form.
Specifically returning to a home base (home, hometown, country) — distinct from 戻る (modoru), returning to any earlier place.
Takes に for the vehicle: 電車に乗ります ('board the train'), not を.
Covers both teaching and simply informing — 電話番号を教えてください is 'please tell me your phone number', not 'teach me'.
Learning through instruction, implying a teacher. Broader self-directed learning is 勉強する or 覚える.
'I know' is the progressive 知っています, not 知ります; but 'I don't know' is the plain negative 知りません — an asymmetry worth memorizing early.
Pairs with と to quote a thought: いいと思います ('I think it's good').
Written いう but usually pronounced 'yuu'. The past 言った is 'itta', a common set form.
Takes に for what's entered: 部屋に入る ('enter the room'). Looks like an ichidan verb but is godan — 入ります, 入って.
Takes を for the place left: 家を出る ('leave the house') — an exception where を marks a point of departure, not an object.
Careful with vowel length: 病院 (byouin, hospital) vs 美容院 (biyouin, hair salon) differ only by a mora.
The everyday word. A more polite option when asking in public is お手洗い (otearai, literally 'hand-washing place').
A clipped loan from 'department store' — Japanese frequently shortens long borrowings this way.
Shortened from 'convenience store' — the full コンビニエンスストア is almost never said.
Literally 'underground iron' (地下 underground + 鉄 iron/rail).
The same kanji 本 is also the counter for long cylindrical things (three pens = 三本 sanbon) and means 'origin/main' in compounds — 日本 is literally 'sun-origin'.
電話する means 'to phone (someone)' — a する-verb built straight from the noun.
Short for 携帯電話 ('portable phone'). Younger speakers increasingly say スマホ (sumaho) for a smartphone.
Blended from 'personal computer' — a made-in-Japan contraction (和製英語) you won't hear in English.
Used for Western-style hinged doors; a traditional sliding door is a 戸 (to) or 障子 (shouji).
Almost always said with the polite お-; bare 金 (kane) alone sounds blunt or greedy.
For transport/admission tickets. A concert or event ticket is more often チケット (chiketto).

examples.text: 今日はいい天気ですね。
examples.reading: きょうはいいてんきですね。
examples.romaji: Kyou wa ii tenki desu ne.

naturalMeaning: 雨 (ame, rain) and 飴 (ame, candy) are homophones distinguished by pitch accent — rain is high-low, candy low-high.
風 (kaze, wind) sounds identical to 風邪 (kaze, a cold/illness) — 風邪をひく is 'to catch a cold'.
Also means chicken as meat — 焼き鳥 (yakitori) is grilled chicken skewers.
語 (go) attaches to a country/people to name its language: 英語 (eigo, English), 中国語 (chuugokugo, Chinese).
Read both にほん (nihon) and にっぽん (nippon) — にほん is the everyday choice, にっぽん more formal/emphatic (sports cheers, official contexts).
仕事をする means 'to work' generally; 働く is the single-word verb for the same idea.
Read とし as the standalone word (年を取る, 'to age') but ねん (nen) as the year-counter (2024年).
一番 (ichiban) literally 'number one' also means 'the most / best' — 一番好き is 'like the most'.
